text.title: WebSocket Client: Reconnect and Message Queue
text.summary: TypeScript client with exponential backoff + full jitter, bounded offline message queue, and subscription lifecycle management.

rule.statement: Implement reconnect with exponential backoff and full jitter: delay = random(0, min(cap, base * 2^n)). Cap retries to a maximum attempt count.
rule.rationale: Reconnect without jitter causes thundering herds: all clients reconnect simultaneously after a server restart, creating a DoS on the auth endpoint. Full jitter spreads reconnect attempts uniformly.

example.code: // No jitter — all clients reconnect at the same time after a deploy setTimeout(() => this.connect(), 1000 * this.reconnectAttempts);
example.note: Self-DDoS in prod: 10k clients all hit /ws-ticket simultaneously.

rule.statement: Buffer outbound messages when disconnected. Cap the queue at a fixed size (e.g., 100 messages) and drop the oldest when full.
rule.rationale: An unbounded queue accumulates indefinitely during outages; when the connection restores, the client floods the server with stale messages.

rule.statement: The subscribe() method must return an unsubscribe function that removes the local handler and sends an unsubscribe message to the server. Clean up on component unmount.
rule.rationale: Leaked subscriptions accumulate channel listeners, preventing GC and delivering stale data to unmounted components.

antipattern.description: Storing session tokens or JWTs in query string parameters of the WebSocket URL.
antipattern.reason: Query strings appear in server access logs and proxy logs; credentials leak immediately.

antipattern.description: No rate limit per connection — one bad client consumes an entire CPU core.
antipattern.reason: WebSocket message handlers run in the same event loop; a client flooding messages starves all other connections.

text.reference: TypeScript WebSocketClient with reconnect, queue, subscribe
